title: SEGMENT 10 — Tropism and re-targeting: fiber knob modifications

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Tropism and re-targeting: fiber knob modifications
Welcome back.

We left off talking about CAR as the natural entry receptor for most human adenoviral types, and we noted that CAR's tissue distribution doesn't perfectly match what we'd like a therapeutic vector to target.
Cancer cells, blood cells, neurons — these are all cells you might want to deliver to, and they all express CAR poorly.
So the obvious engineering question is: can we change what receptor the virus uses, by modifying the capsid surface?
The answer is yes, and there are several distinct strategies, all of which intervene on the fiber protein.
Recall that the fiber is the long stalk-and-knob protein that sticks out from each of the twelve corners of the icosahedral capsid, and the knob at the tip is what binds CAR.
If we modify the knob, we modify the binding specificity, and we modify the tropism.
Strategy one: insert a targeting peptide into the knob.
The knob has several surface-exposed loops, particularly the so-called HI loop, that can tolerate the insertion of short peptide sequences without destroying the overall knob fold.
If you insert, say, an RGD peptide into the HI loop, the modified knob now binds integrins via the inserted peptide in addition to whatever it was binding via CAR.
The vector now has dual tropism — it can enter cells via CAR or via integrins — and on cells that lack CAR but express integrins, it can now enter where it couldn't before.
Different inserted peptides target different cells.
There are peptides that target the asialoglycoprotein receptor on hepatocytes, peptides that target tumor-associated antigens, peptides that target endothelial cells.
Strategy two: swap the entire fiber knob for a different serotype's knob.
Different adenoviral types use different primary receptors.
Ad5 uses CAR, but Ad3 and Ad11 and Ad35 use a different receptor called CD46, which is upregulated on many tumor cells.
So you can construct a chimeric vector — an Ad5 capsid with an Ad35 fiber knob, often called Ad5/35 — that has the manufacturing and genomic backbone of Ad5 but the receptor specificity of Ad35.
Such chimeric vectors transduce tumor cells and hematopoietic stem cells far more efficiently than wild Ad5, because they bind CD46 rather than CAR.
Strategy three: ablate the natural binding and add a new ligand.
This is more aggressive: you make point mutations in the knob that destroy CAR binding entirely, and you make mutations in the penton base that destroy RGD-integrin engagement.
The resulting vector has no natural tropism.
Then you fuse a new targeting ligand — a single-chain antibody, a designed binder, an affibody — to a different part of the capsid, often the C-terminus of the modified fiber.
Now the only way the vector can bind a cell is through your engineered ligand, which gives you exquisite tropism specificity for whatever the ligand targets.
The trade-off is that the engineered targeting often has lower affinity than the natural fiber-CAR interaction, so transduction efficiency drops.
The analogy.
Re-targeting is changing the truck's destination label.
A default Ad5 truck delivers to every house with a CAR mailbox out front.
You can paste a new label onto the bumper that says "also deliver to houses with integrins on the porch.
" You can rebuild the whole truck with a different company's logo so it gets routed to a different set of warehouses.

Or you can strip off all the default labels and put on a single, very specific one — "deliver only to houses with this exact serial number on the door" — which gives you precision at the cost of speed.
The re-targeting field has been active for over twenty years and has produced a lot of beautiful engineering.
But the practical clinical impact has been modest.
In part because re-engineered tropism is only one piece of the puzzle — you still have to deal with pre-existing immunity, with capsid-driven innate response, with cargo capacity.

In part because for many applications, you can achieve the targeting you want by changing the route of delivery rather than the vector — directly injecting into a tumor, for instance, mostly solves the targeting problem without any vector engineering at all.
Re-targeting remains an active area, particularly for oncolytic adenoviruses (Segment 15), but it has not transformed the field the way some early enthusiasts hoped.
